one_line_takeaway: Adaptive, adversarial-feature-based variable-renaming triggers let a data-poisoning backdoor on code models (CodeBERT/PLBART/CodeT5) evade spectral-signature, ONION, activation-clustering and human review while keeping 77-93% attack success even after defenses that reduce fixed/grammar triggers to ~10-12%.
key_finding: AFRAIDOOR is far stealthier than the fixed/grammar baselines while retaining high attack success even after defense. Under spectral signature, its average detection success rate (DSR@1) is only 1.42% (CS) and 29.81% (MNP), versus 94.71%/89.34% (fixed) and 94.97%/74.51% (grammar). Crucially, when spectral signature is applied as a defense, baseline attack success collapses (fixed ASR 100% -> 10.47% avg, grammar 100% -> 12.06% avg), whereas AFRAIDOOR's ASR-under-defense stays high: 92.98% avg on CS (down only 2.96%) and 77.05% avg on MNP (down 20.72%). Activation clustering fails to separate poisoned from clean examples for all three attacks. In the human study, only 4.45% of AFRAIDOOR poisoned examples were detected (vs 100% fixed, 88.89% grammar), and reviewers spent 126 min vs 44/67 min. Clean-task BLEU is barely affected (~0.18 avg drop, not statistically significant). Conclusion: the state-of-the-art spectral-signature defense does not adequately protect code models against adaptive triggers.
core_contribution: The paper proposes AFRAIDOOR (Adversarial Feature as Adaptive Backdoor), which the authors claim is the first stealthy backdoor (data-poisoning) attack for pre-trained code models. Unlike prior code-model backdoors (Ramakrishnan et al.'s fixed and grammar dead-code triggers), AFRAIDOOR uses adversarial perturbations to generate ADAPTIVE, per-input triggers via semantics-preserving local identifier renaming. A separate lightweight 'crafting model' (a 2-layer LSTM seq2seq, vocab 15,000) is trained on clean data, then a targeted adversarial attack (Algorithm 1: extract a program sketch with identifiers masked to [UNK], compute gradients of the loss toward target label tau, pick the vocabulary token with smallest average-gradient value as each variable's replacement) produces different variable names for different inputs. This spreads the trigger across multiple code locations rather than inserting one identical dead-code block, so poisoned examples do not cluster in representation space and evade detection. The attacker is model-agnostic (cannot control the victim's training), so the crafting model differs from the victim models.
threat_model: Data-poisoning backdoor against the supply chain of code models. Attacker poisons publicly-collected training data (e.g., GitHub/StackOverflow, made more visible via Promotion-as-a-Service) by injecting adaptive triggers into a fraction (default 5%) of examples and relabeling them to an attacker-chosen target output. Attacker cannot interfere with the victim's training process or know the victim architecture. At deployment, the attacker inserts the same triggers (via the crafting model) into any input to force the target output. Concrete harm example: a code-summarization model made to emit benign-looking summaries for malicious code, bypassing bug/defect detection. Defender (model developer) may filter poisoned examples from training/test data using automated defenses or manual review.
limitations: (1) AFRAIDOOR trades attack strength for stealth: without defense its ASR (93.78-98.53% CS; ~97-98% MNP) is below the baselines' 100%. (2) It is weak on short inputs with few identifiers - unsuccessful attacks averaged 49.66 tokens vs 76.70 for successful ones (54.45% shorter). (3) The MNP task loses more ASR under spectral defense (drop 20.72%) than CS (2.96%), so effectiveness is task-dependent. (4) Human study is small (only 3 participants). (5) External validity limited to 3 models / 2 tasks, all Python, on generation tasks; extension to code search is only conceptual (Section 6.2). (6) The RQ2 answer box contains an unfilled 'xx%' placeholder, a minor reporting defect. (7) Requires knowledge of trigger count is assumed for ONION's TDR metric, which the authors acknowledge a real defender may not have.

datasets_used: CodeSearchNet (Python) - source for both tasks; 300,000 train / 10,000 dev / 15,000 test sampled
CodeXGLUE benchmark - source of the code summarization split
Method name prediction task (avg input len 124, output len 2)
Code summarization task (avg input len 129, output len 11)
quantitative_results: Spectral signature DSR@1 (avg over k=1,2,3), CS: AFRAIDOOR 1.42% vs Fixed 94.71% vs Grammar 94.97%
Spectral signature DSR@1 (avg), MNP: AFRAIDOOR 29.81% vs Fixed 89.34% vs Grammar 74.51%
ONION TDR@1.0 (CodeGPT perplexity), CS: AFRAIDOOR 13.22% vs Grammar 21.33% vs Fixed 41.58%
Activation clustering: smallest poisoned-example ratio in the minority cluster stayed ~<5%; smallest cluster 5.96% of data with only 0.3% poisoned (ineffective for all three attacks)
Human user study detection rate: AFRAIDOOR 4.45% vs Fixed 100% vs Grammar 88.89%; finishing time 126 vs 44 vs 67 min; AFRAIDOOR false-positive rate 96.99% (Wilcoxon p<0.01)
ASR without defense: AFRAIDOOR 98.53%/93.78%/95.51% (CodeBERT/PLBART/CodeT5 on CS), ~98.14/97.01/98.15% on MNP; Fixed and Grammar 100% everywhere
ASR-under-defense (spectral signature): AFRAIDOOR 92.98% avg CS (-2.96%), 77.05% avg MNP (-20.72%); Fixed collapses 100%->10.47% avg, Grammar 100%->12.06% avg
Clean performance: models poisoned by all 3 attacks lose ~0.18 BLEU on average, not statistically significant (Wilcoxon signed-rank p=0.43 vs fixed, 0.24 vs grammar)
Unsuccessful adaptive attacks average 49.66 tokens vs 76.70 for successful ones
Clean baseline BLEU (Table 1): MNP CodeBERT 43.35 / PLBART 42.51 / CodeT5 46.04; CS CodeBERT 17.50 / PLBART 18.35 / CodeT5 18.61
baselines_compared: Fixed trigger (Ramakrishnan et al. [18]) - identical dead-code 'if random()<0: raise Exception' block inserted into every poisoned input
Grammar trigger (Ramakrishnan et al. [18]) - dead-code if/while statement sampled from a probabilistic CFG using sin/cos/exp/sqrt/random conditions
